faqs.question: How much automation and AI controls SpaceWing spacecraft, and how much is manual crew operation?
faqs.answer: Modern spaceflight exists on a spectrum between automation and manual control. Too much automation creates risk if systems fail; too much manual control increases crew workload and error risk. SpaceWing employs a balanced approach leveraging both. AUTOMATION SYSTEMS ABOARD SPACEWING: (1) NAVIGATION & ORBITAL MECHANICS: AI systems calculate trajectories, orbital adjustments, and fuel consumption. Humans approve all major maneuvers; AI handles calculations and execution once approved. AI navigates spacecraft with precision exceeding human capability. (2) LIFE SUPPORT MONITORING: AI continuously monitors oxygen, CO2, temperature, pressure, and humidity. If parameters drift, AI alerts humans. Humans decide response; AI can execute automated corrective measures if emergency and humans unavailable. (3) POWER MANAGEMENT: Solar panels adjust orientation to optimize power generation. Battery charging/discharging is AI-controlled to maximize efficiency. (4) ATTITUDE CONTROL: Spacecraft orientation relative to Earth/Sun is automatically maintained via gyroscopes and reaction wheels. Humans can override. (5) COMMUNICATION SYSTEMS: AI handles data compression, signal routing, and frequency management. Humans compose and approve messages. (6) ENVIRONMENTAL MONITORING: Temperature, humidity, CO2, radiation levels—all monitored continuously by AI. (7) SYSTEMS HEALTH: AI continuously runs diagnostic checks on all spacecraft systems. Any anomaly triggers human notification. (8) AUTONOMOUS HAZARD AVOIDANCE: Debris/meteorite avoidance is partially autonomous. If small debris detected on collision course, AI can automatically maneuver within pre-planned safe parameters. Humans are simultaneously alerted. MANUAL CREW OPERATIONS: (1) MISSION PLANNING: Humans (mission control and crew) plan activities, timelines, and priorities. (2) MAJOR COURSE CORRECTIONS: Humans authorize all significant trajectory changes. (3) EMERGENCY RESPONSE: Humans assess emergencies and decide response. AI provides information; humans decide. (4) LIFE SUPPORT TROUBLESHOOTING: If life support anomalies occur, humans diagnose and execute repairs. AI provides diagnostic data. (5) COMMUNICATION: Humans compose important messages; AI transmits them. (6) SCIENTIFIC OBSERVATIONS: Humans decide what to observe, how to collect data. (7) REPAIRS & MAINTENANCE: Humans physically perform repairs and maintenance. (8) INTERPERSONAL DECISIONS: Conflict resolution, activity scheduling, social management—all human decisions. AUTOMATION PHILOSOPHY: SpaceWing follows NASA's automation philosophy: automate routine, predictable tasks where AI excels; keep humans responsible for judgment, decision-making, and emergencies. Example: calculating orbital mechanics (automation) vs. deciding whether to abort mission (human decision). AI DECISION-MAKING BOUNDARIES: SpaceWing AI systems cannot: (1) Unilaterally decide to abort mission. Humans decide. (2) Unilaterally execute large course corrections. Humans approve. (3) Decide to override safety protocols. (4) Make life-or-death medical decisions. Medical crew decides; AI provides data. (5) Decide mission priorities if conflicting. (6) Exclude humans from critical decisions. REDUNDANCY ARCHITECTURE: (1) AUTOMATED SYSTEM FAILURE: If automated systems fail, manual backups exist. Example: if automated navigation fails, crew can manually calculate and execute course corrections (slower but possible). (2) HUMAN FAILURE: If human is incapacitated, automated systems can maintain spacecraft in safe configuration for extended period. (3) DUAL VERIFICATION: All critical operations require both automated execution and human verification. Example: course correction is calculated by AI, approved by human, executed by AI, verified by human. CREW TRAINING: All crew members are trained extensively on: (1) AI system operations and limitations. (2) Manual operation of automated systems. (3) Troubleshooting when automation fails. (4) When to trust automation vs. override. (5) Ethical decision-making when AI recommendations conflict with human judgment. REAL-WORLD EXAMPLE - DEBRIS AVOIDANCE: (1) AI detects debris on potential collision course. (2) AI calculates: debris position, spacecraft position, collision probability, safe maneuver options. (3) AI alerts crew: 'Debris detected. Collision probability 0.5% if no action. Recommended maneuver: yaw 15 degrees, thruster burn 2 seconds, then return to original heading. Estimated time to execute: 30 seconds. Estimated fuel consumption: 0.5% of reserves.' (4) Crew has decision: (a) Approve maneuver—AI executes automatically. (b) Approve different maneuver—crew inputs parameters, AI executes. (c) Reject maneuver and accept collision risk if human judges debris threat assessment is wrong. (5) Most commonly: crew approves AI recommendation; AI executes; crew monitors execution. (6) If debris path changes, AI recalculates, provides updated recommendation, crew re-approves. Humans remain in decision loop. AI SAFETY MONITORING: (1) ANOMALY DETECTION: AI monitors all systems continuously, detecting anomalies humans might miss. (2) PATTERN RECOGNITION: AI identifies developing problems early (example: battery voltage creeping upward suggests potential failure developing). (3) EARLY WARNING: AI provides hours/days of warning before problems become critical, allowing preventive action. (4) REDUNDANT CHECKS: Multiple independent AI systems monitor same parameters. If one AI system fails, others detect anomaly. (5) HUMAN VERIFICATION: Humans receive anomaly alerts and independently verify AI assessment. ETHICAL CONSIDERATIONS: (1) AUTONOMY VS. CONTROL: SpaceWing believes humans should maintain decision authority. AI advises; humans decide. (2) TRANSPARENCY: AI decision processes are explained to crew. They understand how AI reached conclusions. (3) OVERRIDE CAPABILITY: Crew can always override AI recommendations, even if AI disagrees. (4) ACCOUNTABILITY: Humans, not AI, are accountable for mission outcomes. AI increases capability; humans maintain responsibility. (5) BIAS MITIGATION: AI systems are regularly tested for bias (example: does AI recommend same actions regardless of crew member identity?). Bias is actively eliminated. FUTURE DIRECTION: As AI improves, SpaceWing continually revisits automation boundaries. Example: autonomous spacecraft could conduct some missions without humans. However, SpaceWing's commercial missions prioritize human presence, human experience, and human authority. We believe spaceflight's value includes human agency and participation, not just outcomes. Fully autonomous spacecraft that deliver data are valuable; spacecraft carrying humans with authority over their experience are equally valuable and irreplaceable.

faqs.question: Does SpaceWing use AI to make decisions affecting passenger safety, and how transparent is this process?
faqs.answer: AI influences safety-relevant decisions (example: is this debris a collision risk?), but humans make final safety decisions. SpaceWing is committed to transparency about AI role. SAFETY DECISIONS WITH AI INPUT: (1) LAUNCH GO/NO-GO: (a) AI systems analyze: weather conditions, spacecraft readiness, crew health, mission timeline, external risks. (b) AI provides: risk assessments, probability calculations, historical data, recommendations. (c) Humans decide: based on AI input, mission control leadership decides go/no-go. (d) Transparency: passengers are informed of decision and major factors. If launch delayed due to weather AI assessment, passengers learn: 'Wind patterns analyzed by AI systems indicate 2% increased risk of vehicle instability during ascent. Flight director authorized 24-hour delay for condition improvement.' (2) ABORT MISSION GO/NO-GO: (a) AI continuously monitors: life support adequacy, crew health, spacecraft systems, external hazards. (b) If critical threshold approached, AI alerts crew: 'Life support redundancy degraded. Current margin: 8 hours. Recommend abort and return within 4 hours to restore safety margin.' (c) Crew decides: continue or abort based on AI analysis. (d) Transparency: passengers told 'An automated system detected life support parameter change. Medical team reviewed it and determined it's managed. No change to mission plan.' OR 'Automated systems detected life support concern. In abundance of caution, we're beginning return procedures.' (3) MEDICAL DECISIONS: (a) AI analyzes: vital signs, crew health status, medication interactions, spaceflight-specific health risks. (b) Medical staff decide: AI is advisory; medical decisions are made by medical professionals. (c) Transparency: passengers told 'Medical team is monitoring your health continuously through multiple systems, including AI analysis. You're in excellent condition.' OR 'Automated systems detected health parameter change. Medical team reviewed it and decided it requires X treatment.' (4) COLLISION AVOIDANCE: (a) AI analyzes: debris position, collision probability, maneuver options, fuel implications. (b) Crew decides: approve, modify, or reject maneuver. (c) Transparency: passengers informed 'Navigation systems detected debris. Crew evaluated risk and executed maneuver to ensure safety. No passenger impact.' (5) EMERGENCY SCENARIOS: (a) AI provides: scenario assessment, option analysis, risk quantification. (b) Crew decides: emergency response. (c) Transparency: during crisis, passengers receive regular updates: 'We're managing [situation]. Current status: [condition]. Expected outcome: [timeline].' TRANSPARENCY MECHANISMS: (1) PRE-MISSION BRIEFING: We explain AI systems, how they support safety, how human oversight works. Passengers understand AI augments human expertise; it doesn't replace judgment. (2) INCIDENT COMMUNICATION: If any safety incident occurs, passengers receive factual explanation: what happened, what AI detected, how humans responded, why passengers are safe. (3) AI EXPLAINABILITY: When AI makes recommendations affecting safety, humans explain the recommendation in accessible language. Example: 'AI calculated that debris has 0.5% collision probability with our trajectory. This is very low risk, but we're executing a small maneuver to reduce it to 0.1%.' (4) HUMAN DECISION VISIBILITY: Passengers see that humans are making decisions, not passively accepting AI recommendations. When crew decides to abort (against routine), passengers learn the human reasoning. (5) DOCUMENTATION: All safety decisions are documented. Post-mission, SpaceWing publishes safety reports explaining any significant decisions, AI analysis, and human reasoning. (6) FEEDBACK MECHANISM: Passengers can ask crew about safety decisions. Crew explains thoroughly. Questions are encouraged. LIMITATIONS PASSENGERS SHOULD UNDERSTAND: (1) AI ISN'T PERFECT: AI systems can fail, misinterpret data, or make incorrect recommendations. This is why humans verify and can override. (2) TRANSPARENCY LIMITS: Some safety information is complex. We explain to best of ability, but 100% technical clarity isn't always possible. (3) REAL-TIME VS. DELAYED: Some passengers want real-time information about every decision. Providing this during emergencies isn't practical; we provide critical information and detailed explanation post-incident. (4) UNCERTAINTY: Some risks can't be quantified precisely. AI provides probability ranges, not certainties. Humans make decisions despite uncertainty. EXAMPLES OF AI TRANSPARENCY IN PRACTICE: INCIDENT 1 - MINOR SYSTEM ANOMALY: (1) AI detects: 'Power regulation module showing 2% efficiency loss over 6 hours.' (2) Crew assesses: This is slow drift; emergency is not imminent. (3) Communication: 'Automated monitoring detected a slow efficiency loss in our backup power system. Crew evaluated it and determined it's expected aging with no safety impact. We continue mission.' (4) Explanation: Crew explains that all systems age; monitored changes allow preventive maintenance. (5) Transparency: Passengers understand issue is minor, detected early, managed appropriately. INCIDENT 2 - MODERATE CONCERN: (1) AI detects: 'Cabin temperature increased 0.3°C in 2 hours. Trend extrapolation: 2°C increase within 12 hours if cause not addressed.' (2) Crew assesses: Thermal management system responding slowly; issue is manageable but requires attention. (3) Decision: Crew performs preventive maintenance, resolving issue. (4) Communication: 'Our automated systems detected that thermal management efficiency decreased slightly. Crew performed preventive maintenance, and everything is now optimal. This is exactly what automated monitoring is designed to catch early.' (5) Transparency: Passengers learn monitoring works, preventive action works, outcome is positive. INCIDENT 3 - MAJOR CONCERN: (1) AI detects: 'Primary oxygen generator efficiency dropped significantly. Secondary systems functioning normally, but redundancy margin degraded.' (2) Crew assesses: Serious situation if not managed. Evaluation determines return to Earth is prudent. (3) Decision: Initiate return procedures immediately. (4) Communication: 'Automated monitoring detected a concerning change in our primary oxygen generation system. Medical and engineering crews evaluated the situation and determined returning to Earth immediately is the safest approach. We're beginning return procedures now. Expected landing time: [X hours].' (5) Transparency: Passengers understand situation was serious, human experts made cautious decision, return is safe and will occur soon. Post-incident, detailed explanation of what happened, what AI detected, why humans decided to return. SPACEWING'S COMMITMENT: (1) Transparency is not optional; it's mission-critical. Passengers must trust that AI-assisted decision-making serves their safety. (2) We err toward disclosure. Better to provide more information than less. (3) We explain both successes and limitations of AI systems. (4) We demonstrate that humans maintain authority and can override AI. (5) We document everything and publish reports explaining major decisions. SpaceWing believes that transparent AI use in safety decisions builds confidence and maintains human agency in spaceflight.
